operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+OpenCog (1508681e37a3984f)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: OpenCog
Messages: 5
Date range: 2015-10-20 to 21 Oct 2015
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 1508681e37a3984f

============================================================

--- Message 1/5 ---
From: Manvi Garg
Date: 2015-10-20

Hello, I am a 3rd year undergraduate student from IIT Kharagpur. I am working on projects based on NLP, machine learning and ROS. Can you tell me how to start contributing to the code?

--- Message 2/5 ---
From: Aleks TK
Date: 2015-10-20

You should check out the wiki and grab a copy of Engineering General Intelligence, a link is on the wiki. You should also join the IRC channel and grab a copy of the source code.

--- Message 3/5 ---
From: Amen Belayneh
Date: 20 Oct 2015

Hi and welcome, with regards to development environment you can take a look at http://wiki.opencog.org/w/Building_OpenCog. If you choose to use ubuntu you can take a look at http://wiki.opencog.org/wikihome/index.php/Ocpkg for installing dependencies.

--- Message 4/5 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-10-21

Hey Amen, for ROS, it would be cool if we could get some version of ocpkg that also set up everything needed to get the minecraft world working. That way, we could point newcomers at that.

-- Linas

--- Message 5/5 ---
From: Amen Belayneh
Date: 21 Oct 2015

Hi Linas, Good idea. Mandeep is just starting with Minecraft, he will be adding the configuration steps to ocpkg as well as dockerizing it.

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+Opencog coding and development standards while editing existing code (14bbdbf2bfa0e014)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: Opencog coding and development standards while editing existing code
Messages: 5
Date range: 2015-02-24 to 2015-02-25
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14bbdbf2bfa0e014

============================================================

--- Message 1/5 ---
From: Curtis M. Faith
Date: 2015-02-24

I find it nontrivially more difficult to quickly parse new code when coding style around control statements is not consistent. Much of the existing code does not follow the development standards. What should we do with existing code? Fix whole file before a Pull Request? Fix only functions we touch?

--- Message 2/5 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-02-24

Please do not change whitespace. Except maybe for the missing whitespace after control statements.

--- Message 3/5 ---
From: Curtis Faith
Date: 2015-02-24

Sorry for the confusion, I am not proposing changing whitespace in existing files except for control statements. I was only asking about code that I write myself.

--- Message 4/5 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-02-24

Ideally, follow the style that is established in the current file.

--- Message 5/5 ---
From: Nil Geisweiller
Date: 2015-02-25

I have added a line about space between if and ( on the wiki. I personally feel both brace styles are OK. During a debate I browsed the code of about two dozen projects, coding standards are very poorly respected in large projects. If you wish to replace all if/for/while( by if/for/while (, I do not think that would bother anyone.

operations.name: RawData+opencog-ml+OpenCog Development Standards - CamelCase or under_lines (14bdb34d3c05f5d6)
operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: OpenCog Development Standards - CamelCase or under_lines
Messages: 15
Date range: 2 Mar 2015 to 4 Mar 2015
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14bdb34d3c05f5d6

============================================================

--- Message 1/15 ---
From: Curtis M. Faith
Date: 2 Mar 2015

The document:

http://wiki.opencog.org/w/Development_standards

does not address style for names of identifiers, i.e. CamelCase or 
under_lines. Most of the code I read seems to use CamelCase, but I've not 
begun to have read enough to feel comfortable that this is representative 
 [...]

--- Message 2/15 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-03-02

I dislike camel case. Its hard to read, visually. (i.e. a usability issue).

--linas

On Mon, Mar 2, 2015 at 9:57 AM, Curtis M. Faith <curtis.m.faith@gmail.com>
wrote:

--- Message 3/15 ---
From: Nil Geisweiller
Date: 2015-03-03

I'm glad you bring the topic, Curtis.

I recently came to the same conclusion, camelCase is more compact but is 
harder to read.

Plus, both STL and boost use under_lines, and we heavily rely on them.

So I'm in favor of adding that to the standard. And even progressively 
convert the code into under_lines (at least the user APIs for starter).

Nil

--- Message 4/15 ---
From: Nil Geisweiller
Date: 2015-03-03

It's interesting they combine both camelCase and under_lines actually

http://en.cppreference.com/w/cpp/io/basic_ofstream
http://en.cppreference.com/w/cpp/io/basic_ios/fill
http://www.boost.org/doc/libs/1_57_0/libs/algorithm/doc/html/header/boost/algorithm/cxx11/any_of_hpp.html

It seems only generic types found in template are in camelCase, I think 
I like it.

I'll try to find a bit more about that and update the wiki.

Nil

--- Message 5/15 ---
From: Nil Geisweiller
Date: 2015-03-03

I took the freedom to add the following

http://wiki.opencog.org/w/Development_standards#Multi-word_identifiers

But ultimately it's very much a question of taste. Using camelCase has 
also pros, see

http://code-cruising.net/underscore-vs-camelcase-naming-convention

According to my own taste and experience, and the fact that we're using 
a lot of STL and boost code, I suggest to settle to underscore. Open to 
discussion of course.

Nil

--- Message 6/15 ---
From: Nil Geisweiller
Date: 2015-03-03

Hmm, I'm not sure we want to turn

AtomSpace atomspace;

into

atomspace atomspace;

But we may want to turn

as.getHandle(CONCEPT_NODE, "abc");

into

as.get_handle(CONCEPT_NODE, "abc");

I've update the wiki accordingly.

Nil

--- Message 7/15 ---
From: Curtis Faith
Date: 2015-03-03

​Yes, it is a matter of taste to some extent what the particular standard
is. Though it is not a matter of taste that one exists.

When I come to a project and see two or three or more styles within one
file, or within the headers, it says to me that the project is not very
 [...]

--- Message 8/15 ---
From: Nil Geisweiller
Date: 2015-03-03

Thanks very much for your input, Curtis. Yeah, I'm with you on the many 
points you raise.

An intermediate level of standard sounds right.

So I think we need to move forward regarding

1. The standard itself (of course any suggestion you may have are 
greatly appreciated)

2. Enforcing the standard. Cause even our under-specified standard isn't 
well respected, making it more complex isn't gonna solve that. So here 
again, ideas to address that is welcome.

What I might suggest would be to have some script that can check whether 
a commit violates the standard. Then, I *guess*, github could have a 
callback to that script so that branches can be checked before being merged.

BTW, this github callback thing could be used for running test, etc. 
 [...]

--- Message 9/15 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-03-03

Our class names are CamelCase, and that's fine by me.  That way, you can
always spot a class instantly: you know its CamelCase.

The more visually different cues, the better. so: CamelCase type and class
names, and under_scored function names is a comfortable place for me.

I also like: class members start with an underscore.  I notice that some
people like to write this->member to make it clear that member is a member
and not a local variable. For me, this->member seems clunky and verbose,
whereas _member strikes a happy balance.

--linas

On Tue, Mar 3, 2015 at 3:47 AM, Nil Geisweiller <ngeiswei@googlemail.com>
wrote:

--- Message 10/15 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-03-03

Yowie.  Curtis, we have agreed and seen eye to eye on almost everything
till now, but the philosophy below .. it is a major turn-off.  For me, it
just provokes my worst-possible anti-authoritarian knee-jerk reaction.  You
really don't want to go there. I really don't want to go there.

--line

On Tue, Mar 3, 2015 at 9:29 AM, Curtis Faith <curtis.m.faith@gmail.com>
wrote:

--- Message 11/15 ---
From: Curtis Faith
Date: 2015-03-03

Linas, won't you be happier if new contributors always use:

"CamelCase type and class names, and under_scored function names, and
even '_member'

rather than some random combination of the above?

That will make it a lot easier for you to know what is going on and the
code won't be as clunky. [...]

--- Message 12/15 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-03-04

Hi Curtis, etc.,

At risk of overcomplicating things, I wonder if it might be useful to
distinguish two sets of development standards...

1)  Strict development standards.   These are rules that we really
really want everyone to follow when putting code into the OpenCog
codebase.   This will be pretty basic stuff, and every major
contributor to OpenCog has obviously got to agree on these.   These
rules should be pretty strongly enforced, so that if someone submits
code breaking these standards, the code can be rejected.  (Of course,
if the person has a good reason for violating the standard in some
particular case, we're not dumb robots and can make exceptions.....)

2) Development guidelines.   These are suggestions that we make, aimed
 [...]

--- Message 13/15 ---
From: Nil Geisweiller
Date: 2015-03-04

Yeah, migrating the least important standards rules to guidelines could 
be worthwhile. On the other hand, as of today, our development standards 
is perhaps still simple enough that everything can be enforced.

Perhaps the definition-block brackets and spaces between operators could 
be added as guidelines, while, indentation (smart-tab or 4 spaces, as 
long as it is consistent with the same file), 80-col, unix-style EOL and 
space between if and condition could stay in the standard. (as for 
multi-word identifiers I just don't know).

But what I really would like to see is to have the builtbot checkout 
each pull request, compile it, check the coding style (maybe using 
astyle) and send the report back to us. Then it's up to us to decide 
 [...]

--- Message 14/15 ---
From: Curtis Faith
Date: 2015-03-04

Ben, that seems a good suggestion. The added complexity of a separation
fits the underlying complexity of the issue so it doesn't really introduce
any new complexity.

Interestingly, Boost distinguishes between Requirements and Guidelines:
http://www.boost.org/development/requirements.html

 [...]

--- Message 15/15 ---
From: William Ma
Date: 4 Mar 2015

I would rather see the 80-col max thing be part of the guideline rather 
than the standard.  80-col is too short is most cases and makes the code 
harder to read (for me anyway).

William

--- Message 1/3 ---
From: William Ma
Date: 2015-01-13

Needed for R2L outputs. @AmeBel add this to the DialogueSystem milestone?

--- Message 2/3 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-01-13

Aaron, Are you thinking of tagging each argument-slot of a predicate with an indication of the semantic role? We did that in a previous OpenCog version using FrameNet. The old RelEx2Frame system was problematic, but it could be viable to reintroduce FrameNet in a limited way.

-- Ben

--- Message 3/3 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-01-13

Aaron, if your semantic role labels are as broad as Agent, Theme and Patient, then many verbs have common labels across their senses. We would need to use some database of semantic role labels. PropBank is likely the best choice. This could be replicated using link parses, RelEx and R2L output as features. This would be a pretty good GSoC project.

-- Ben

operations.content:
OpenCog Mailing List Thread: OpenCog wiki revision
Messages: 3
Date range: 2015-03-14 to 2015-03-15
Source: opencog@googlegroups.com
Thread ID: 14c18e603e257d4a

============================================================

--- Message 1/3 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-03-14

Hi all,

In bits and pieces over the last few weeks, I have been working on putting a new 'front section' on the wiki site. The new front page is at http://wiki.opencog.org/ and the meatiest new page is http://wiki.opencog.org/w/AI_Documentation which attempts to give brief, up to date pointers to documentation and code for every significant currently implemented AI function in OpenCog.

I have also linked to the pirate version of Engineering General Intelligence. And the whole book Real World Reasoning is now on the wiki site.

There are something like 1100 wiki pages altogether so cleanup won't be a super fast task.

-- Ben

--- Message 2/3 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-03-14

BTW, I think there are some recent tutorials out there that I haven't linked to Getting_Started_as_an_OpenCog_Developer or to AI_Documentation. If you made or know of tutorials that aren't linked there, please add them.

thx
ben

--- Message 3/3 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-03-15

Ben,

a particularly useful thing to do is to make sure all pages are in some category or another (they can be in multiple categories).

I find this to be an extremely useful way of navigating, as it instantly shows you everything on some given topic. Its also a lot easier to add a category to a page, then it is to edit 10 or 20 other pages to add it to their 'see also' section.

--linas

--- Message 1/3 ---
From: Curtis M. Faith
Date: 31 Mar 2015

In looking at AI and most especially AGI, we are very definitely compute-limited. Improvements will come from increases in parallelization and from single-core algorithmic improvements. [...]

--- Message 2/3 ---
From: Joel Pitt
Date: 2015-04-06

Hi Curtis, Everything you say here makes sense to me. In terms of OpenCog, my own interest has been in trying to ensure dynamics are graph localised. I did something like EMA when I implemented the HebbianUpdatingAgent many years ago.

--- Message 3/3 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-04-09

My attitude right now is that it is more important to create code that works and actually does something interesting, rather than performance-tuning it. For example, if we can build a chatbot that actually can have an intelligent conversation, even at the rate of one sentence a day, that would be astounding.

So my advice: don't go crazy about optimization; making it do something useful is far, far far more important.

--linas

--- Message 1/21 ---
From: Eddie Monroe
Date: 2015-06-10

Repeated pattern matching leaves a lot of "artifact" search links
containing VariableNodes in the atomspace.

Of course can filter these out after the fact, but is there a way to have
the PM exclude results that contain links with VariableNodes? Would a
 [...]

--- Message 2/21 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-06-10

That seems like a useful optional feature to have for the PM....

--- Message 3/21 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-06-10

there are two different solutions: in one, you specify search patterns with
types in them; that way anything of the wrong type is not matched.

The other way is to put the search patterns in a derived atomspace; and use
them to search the main atomsapce.  In principle, the derived space is not
searched .. but perhaps this is buggy; its certainly untested.

The first way is easier, but it does make the patterns a bit more verbose.

--linas

 [...]

From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-06-12

On Fri, Jun 12, 2015 at 10:53 AM, Eddie Monroe <eddiemonroe@gmail.com>
wrote:

Not that I can think of.

The PLN rules should almost surely limit their applicability to
ConceptNodes etc a appropriate.  In general, the atomspace will be littered
with all sorts of crazy node and link types, and PLN should ignore anything
that is not a part of it's domain (such as variable nodes).

--linas

--- Message 8/21 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-06-13

In this case, manually deleting the VariableNodes after the search
seems the best idea...

It's true that PLN will ignore Atom types that it really doesn't care
about -- if you put a FooBarNode in the Atomspace, most PLN rules will
ignore it (though not all, e.g. if you say

MemberLink
   FooBarNode "foobar77"
   ConceptNode "foobars"

then PLN will pay attention to this)....

But PLN does have a lot to say about VariableNodes, so if the
Atomspace has a lot of VariableNodes related to important Atoms, by
its default control policy it's bound to try to do something with
them...

A better PLN control policy, in the rule target selection phase, would
prioritize other Atoms besides VariableNodes by default (unless
certain VariableNodes had a particularly high importance for some
 [...]

--- Message 9/21 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-06-12

well, the PLN rules themselves are in the atomspace, which means that any
variables in the PLN rules are in the atomspace, which means that the PLN
rules will find one-another and try to act on one-another.  I don't know if
that is what you want.

Are you familiar with the concept of a "generator" in mathematics? I
believe that the PLN rules could act like "generators":  so, even if the
atomspace starts empty, without constants, and contains only PLN rules,
then, after only one iteration, it will have (generate or "deduce") all
sequences that are two steps long. After two steps, all sequences that are
three rules long, etc. so  very quickly, the atomspace should fill with all
possible deductions, these having only variables in them.

 [...]

--- Message 10/21 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-06-13

yeah ...

In OpenCog / PLN lingo, "inference control" is the process of
describing which PLN rules to apply at what point in time....   This
process should rely on, among other things, the importance values
(attention values) associated with Atoms, which will be regulated by
ECAN (Economic Attention Allocation) and other methods...

ben

--- Message 11/21 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-06-12

I'm sorry I mentioned inference control. I was trying to talk about
generation.  --linas

--- Message 12/21 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-06-13

Generation is a relevant concept to be thinking of --- but as you
point out, just generating all possible combinations of what's in the
Atomspace is not very useful....  That is where the AI comes in...

--- Message 13/21 ---
From: Linas Vepstas
Date: 2015-06-12

What I'm saying is that, unless you choose to ignore variable nodes, you
will generate all possible combinations of assertions that have variables
in them.

--linas

Understood...

William's workaround does the trick, but is not practical for large

--- Message 20/21 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-06-15

Eddie --

Yes, this has been discussed many times before ... I don;t have time
to dig up the old discussions right now, but the bottom line is that
there is no way to normalize away the complexity and variety....   The
existence of multiple forms just goes with the territor...

For instance

A)
Evaluation
    eat
    cat
    mouse

implies both

B)
Inheritance
     cat
     SatisfyingSet
          Evaluation
              eat
              *
              mouse

and

C)
Inheritance
     mouse
     SatisfyingSet
          Evaluation
              eat
              cat
              *

...

but we don't want to replace A with B or C ...

...

On the other hand, Amen's idea of macro rules makes a lot of sense and
seems viable to me..

-- Ben

From: Ben Goertzel

If anyone has a 6 months to spare, a fun little project would be to implement the OpenCog Pattern Matcher on a GPU using MapGraph: http://mapgraph.io/

This paper causes me to revisit my previous attitude that 'large-scale graph processing and current SIMD GPUs don't mix.' It seems they can if you work hard enough.

-- ben

--- Message 2/2 ---
From: Ben Goertzel
Date: 2015-08-14

It seems very cool but also very difficult -- maybe you can work on that next year once we've gotten over the hurdle of getting our initial dialogue system working!

The way to prepare would be to understand fully what the pattern matcher is doing under the hood.

-- ben

Your background certainly sounds relevant. I think we could use your expertise in the PLN component of OpenCog. I wonder if a good project might be to implement some modal logic rules and truth value formulas in PLN. This would be fairly light on the software engineering and heavy on the logic.

I think the general approach in the referenced AAAI 2007 paper could be adapted to OpenCog, including the idea of using a sampling algorithm to estimate truth values. This seems a promising and feasible direction for a GSoC project.

Welcome to the OpenCog team! As Ben mentioned, you likely know more about modal logic than anyone else on the team.
